coredata.dc:title: Chiral CDs-based fluorescence sensor for rapid and specific sensing K4[Fe(CN)6] in table salt and salted food
coredata.dc:creator: Yin, Xiangyu
Wei, Shanshan
Zhai, Changyu
Wang, Bin
Zhang, Hongyuan
Wang, Chenzhao
Song, Xuewei
Sun, Guoying
Jiang, Chunzhu
coredata.dc:description: Potassium ferricyanide (K4[Fe(CN)6]) as anti-caking agent plays an important role in avoiding the formation of chunks for fine particulate solids. However, inappropriate and excessive addition and decomposition of K4[Fe(CN)6] are detrimental to physical health. At present, appropriate strategies for convenient and accurate analysis of K4[Fe(CN)6] in table salt and pickled food are desirable. Herein, an efficient “ON-OFF-ON” fluorescent sensor based on chiral carbon dots was prepared by a simple one-step hydrothermal method. The chiral CDs with L-Tryptophan and D-Tryptophan as chiral source were named as L-CDs and D-CDs. Notably, the bright fluorescence of L/D-CDs could be effectively quenched by K4[Fe(CN)6] through dynamic quenching mechanism. This fluorescent sensor achieved excellent sensitive and selective detection of K4[Fe(CN)6] with a limit of detection (LOD) of 25.0 ng·mL−1. In addition, the L/D-CDs could be applied not only for selective fluorescent recognition of K4[Fe(CN)6] by the methods of portable filter paper and hydrogels, but also as fluorescent dye for repeated message encryption and decryption.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent sensor
Carbon dot
Potassium ferrocyanide
Table salt

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of K4[Fe(CN)6] solid paper sensor
Application of L-CDs for advanced message encryption

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Jilin Province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Jilin Province Natural Science Foundation ( 20220101350JC ) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( U2241287 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image (Inset: corresponding HR-TEM image and the size distribution of L-CDs); (b) AFM image; (c) the XRD spectra and (d) Raman of L-CDs; (e)The FT-IR spectra of L-Try, CC and L-CDs; (f) The 1H NMR spectra of L-Try and L-CDs (D2O).

(a-c) High-resolution C1s, N1s and O1s spectra and (d) XPS spectrum of L-CDs; (e)UV–vis absorption, excitation and emission spectrums of the as-prepared L-CDs. (Inset: L-CDs solution photographed in daylight (left) and under an ultraviolet lamp (right)); (f) a series of emission spectrums of L-CDs in the λex range of 330–400 nm.

The fluorescence stability of L-CDs (a) in different concentrations of NaCl solution, (b) under 365 UV light; (c)selectivity and (d) competition for L-CDs and potassium ferrocyanide; (e) the fluorescence spectrums of L-CDs after adding potassium ferrocyanide (0.0–55.0 μg·mL−1) (inset: linear relationship between F0/F and the concentration of potassium ferrocyanide (0.0–1.0 μg·mL−1); (f) Linear relationship in the range of 4.0–211.0 μg·mL−1.

(a) The absorption spectrum of potassium ferrocyanide (black curve), excitation (green curve) and emission (blue curve) spectrums of the as-prepared L-CDs; (b) the fluorescence lifetime of L-CDs and L-CDs supplemented with potassium ferrocyanide; (c) the constant value of Ksv between L-CDs and potassium ferrocyanide at different temperatures; (d) dynamic quenching mechanism of L-CDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The schematic diagram of preparation procedure of L-CDs and the dynamic quenching mechanism of potassium ferrocyanide sensing.
Recovery of potassium ferrocyanide in real samples by L-CD.

head.ce:title: Chiral CDs-based fluorescence sensor for rapid and specific sensing K4[Fe(CN)6] in table salt and salted food

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Life Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Potassium ferricyanide (K4[Fe(CN)6]) as anti-caking agent plays an important role in avoiding the formation of chunks for fine particulate solids. However, inappropriate and excessive addition and decomposition of K4[Fe(CN)6] are detrimental to physical health. At present, appropriate strategies for convenient and accurate analysis of K4[Fe(CN)6] in table salt and pickled food are desirable. Herein, an efficient “ON-OFF-ON” fluorescent sensor based on chiral carbon dots was prepared by a simple one-step hydrothermal method. The chiral CDs with L-Tryptophan and D-Tryptophan as chiral source were named as L-CDs and D-CDs. Notably, the bright fluorescence of L/D-CDs could be effectively quenched by K4[Fe(CN)6] through dynamic quenching mechanism. This fluorescent sensor achieved excellent sensitive and selective detection of K4[Fe(CN)6] with a limit of detection (LOD) of 25.0 ng·mL−1. In addition, the L/D-CDs could be applied not only for selective fluorescent recognition of K4[Fe(CN)6] by the methods of portable filter paper and hydrogels, but also as fluorescent dye for repeated message encryption and decryption.

Potassium ferricyanide (K4[Fe(CN)6]) as anti-caking agent plays an important role in avoiding the formation of chunks for fine particulate solids. However, inappropriate and excessive addition and decomposition of K4[Fe(CN)6] are detrimental to physical health. At present, appropriate strategies for convenient and accurate analysis of K4[Fe(CN)6] in table salt and pickled food are desirable. Herein, an efficient “ON-OFF-ON” fluorescent sensor based on chiral carbon dots was prepared by a simple one-step hydrothermal method. The chiral CDs with L-Tryptophan and D-Tryptophan as chiral source were named as L-CDs and D-CDs. Notably, the bright fluorescence of L/D-CDs could be effectively quenched by K4[Fe(CN)6] through dynamic quenching mechanism. This fluorescent sensor achieved excellent sensitive and selective detection of K4[Fe(CN)6] with a limit of detection (LOD) of 25.0 ng·mL−1. In addition, the L/D-CDs could be applied not only for selective fluorescent recognition of K4[Fe(CN)6] by the methods of portable filter paper and hydrogels, but also as fluorescent dye for repeated message encryption and decryption.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Food safety remains a persistent concern in the world.(Xu, Xiao, & Xu, 2023) In daily life, fine particulate solids, such as food-grade salt, flour, coffee, and sugar exhibit a tendency to agglomerate.(Bode et al., 2012) In order to prevent clumping, the addition of anti-caking agents is necessary to maintain the loose structure of these materials for long-term storage.(Wang, Huang, He, Ran, & Song, 2023) Among them, potassium ferricyanide as a kind of familiar food additive is commonly used in table salt and salted food to prevent food from aggregation and denaturation and permit a free-flowing condition.(Gupta, Pel, Steiger, & Kopinga, 2015) However, it is worth noting that improper or excessive use of this additive will have negative effects on physical health.(Li, Liu, Xie, Kong, & Liu, 2007) For example, prolonged inhalation or ingestion of potassium ferrocyanide can bring toxic and irritating effects on the respiratory system, skin, and eyes, even generate potential nerve damage, Parkinson's syndrome and cognitive confusion.(Li et al., 2007) Alarmingly, even micro-doses of potassium ferrocyanide can generate potassium cyanide at high temperature.(Hu et al., 2021) When exposed to acidic/alkaline conditions, it can transform into hazardous hydrogen cyanide or sodium cyanide, and induce coughing, dyspnea, and gastrointestinal distress. According to the report, high doses may cause syncope, loss of consciousness, or even death.(Hu et al., 2020) In order to guarantee the performance and safety of salt and preserved foods, relevant organizations had formulated the specific requirements in the food salt industry, such as the maximum limiting quantity of potassium ferricyanide. The maximum limiting quantity of potassium ferricyanide for food grade salt and its substitutes are 10 mg/kg (China Food and Drug Administration) (Organization, 2013), 14 mg/kg (FAO/WHO Codex Alimentarius Commission) and 20 mg/kg (European Union) (European Commission, 2011), respectively. In view of the food safety, it is crucial to explore a portable and rapid strategy for the sensitive and accurate detecting of potassium ferricyanide in table salt and salt-based foods. At present, many approaches have been applied for the detection of potassium ferricyanide, including the titrimetric method, infrared spectrometry method, high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC)(Lim, Hwang, Choi, Lee, Yoon, & Kim, 2018), Resonance Rayleigh scattering (RRS)(Li et al., 2007) and spectrophotometry methods. Nevertheless, the above analytical methods often exist shortcomings, for example time-consuming, high cost and heavy equipment and complex preprocessing, making them inadequate to meet the rapid, reliable, and portable requirements of food analysis.

Actually, fluorescent probes are promising because of their excellent selectivity, high sensitivity, rapid response and clear imaging.(Yu et al., 2023) CDs, as a kind of fluorescent nanomaterials with excellent water solubility, good biological safety, easy surface modification, controlled photoluminescence (PL) and other advantages, have been widely applied in the fields of biosensing, drug delivery, environmental protection, food analysis, information encryption and photocatalysis, etc. Especially, CDs-based fluorescent sensors were explored for recognition and labeling various specific analytes, such as heavy metal ions, food additives, veterinary and agricultural drug residues.(Xu et al., 2023) For instance, Fan and his colleagues prepared chiral CDs from cane molasses and l-cysteine through a two-step solvothermal process. These CDs were capable of detecting Ag+ through the formation of complexes with chiral CDs.(Fan et al., 2023) Yang et al. prepared N and Cl co-doped fluorescence CDs for the analysis of tartrazine through fluorescence resonance energy transfer mechanism.(Yang, Xu, Luo, Tang, Zhang, & Zhao, 2020) Huang and his colleagues synthesized N-doped CDs for the analysis of the activity of acetylcholinesterase and the presence of organophosphorus pesticides in food and tap water.(Huang, Yao, Chu, Liu, Xiao, & Zhang, 2019) Although CDs have great advantages in food analysis, there are unpredictable challenges in sensitive and selective recognition for complex food matrix. At present, there are rare studies that can quickly and portably detect potassium ferrocyanide. Thus, it is urgent to establish a CDs-based fluorescence sensor with excellent performance for the analysis of food additives.

Herein, we selected L/D-Try and the natural pigment of cyanidin cation (CC) to prepare L/D-CDs with abundant tryptophan residues by one-step hydrothermal method (Scheme 1 ). Particularly, the fluorescence of L/D-CDs was selectively quenched by potassium ferrocyanide, thus an accurate and rapid analysis method was successfully applied to real sample detection. Furthermore, the analytical performance and the fluorescence quenching mechanism of the proposed sensing system were investigated in detail. As the main difference between L-CDs and D-CDs was their chiral optical feature, the characterizations described below were represented by L-CDs. This study developed a novel method for the detection of potassium ferrocyanide with a much lower LOD of 25.0 ng·mL−1 due to the chemical and optical stability and high photoluminescence quantum yield (19.2%) of the chiral L-CDs, opening a new avenue for food quality monitoring.

Potassium ferrocyanide was offered by Tianjin Guangfu Technology Development Co., LTD. L/D-Tryptophan (L/D-Try), cyanidin cation (CC), citric acid (CA), ascorbic acid (AA), phenylalanine (PHE), cysteine (Cys) and glucose (Glu) were bought from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Distilled water was obtained from Mingche Water Purification System Equipment (China). All materials were used without further processing or purification.

The morphology and size of L-CDs were observed by high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM, JEOL-2100F). The topography and height distribution image of L-CDs were recorded on a Multimode VIII atomic force microscope (AFM, Dimension ICON2-SYS) instrument. The Raman spectra was performed on a Raman spectrometer (HORIBA, LABRAM HR Evolution). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were collected on a diffractometer (Rigaku, Smart Lab-9 kw) using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å) with a scanning range of 10° to 80°. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, X’Pert3 MRD) was acquired on a CALAB 250 Xi surface analysis system using Al as the excitation source. The FT-IR spectra were performed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (Thermofisher, Nicolet IS50) with 64 scans from 4000 cm−1 to 500 cm−1. The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were obtained on a UV–vis spectrometer (Varian, Cary50) and a fluorescence spectrometer (Shimadzu, RF-5301PC), respectively. The CD spectra were measured using Chirascan V100 spectrophotometer (Applied Photophysics, Chirascan). The aqueous solution L-CDs were freeze-dried on an FDU-2110 machine to attain a yellow powder.

Chiral L/D-CDs and achiral CDs were successfully prepared using a one-step solvothermal route. Firstly, L/D-Try (0.15 mol) and CC (0.05 mol) were dissolved in ultrapure water and stirred, and then heated at 200 °C for 8 h. Naturally cool to room temperature, the primary products were centrifuged and filtered to remove the particulate carbon residues to acquire chiral L/D-CDs primary liquid. In order to further purify these CDs, the yellow primary liquid was dialyzed with a dialysis bag, and water was changed every 6 h over a period of 18 h. The clear solution was freeze-dried to obtain a yellow solid powder, which was preserved in a refrigerator at 4℃. L/D-Try-CDs (only L-Try (0.15 mol) or D-Try (0.15 mol) present) and achiral CDs (only CC 0.05 mol) were prepared under the same conditions.

The ability of L-CDs to recognize potassium ferrocyanide in the neutral condition was investigated. The potassium ferrocyanide solution of 0.1 mmol·L−1 was prepared by dissolving the appropriate mass of potassium ferrocyanide in ultrapure water at a fixed volume of 50.0 mL in a beaker. Similarly, other interfering ions and small molecular solutions were also using the same procedure. To investigate the selectivity of L-CDs towards potassium ferrocyanide, we added 100.0 μL of L-CDs solution to the predetermined potassium ferrocyanide solution, and the fluorescence changes of the mixed solution were tested. The optical response of various small molecules and ions including NH4 +, K+, Na+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Al3+, F−, Cl−, NO3 –, SO4 2−, Glu, AA, CA, Cys and PHE were also studied by fluorescence spectrometry.

All salt samples and salted food, including refined well salt, algae iodized salt, natural sea salt, salted radish, salted cucumber and pickled cabbage, were obtained from the same store (Changchun, China). Firstly, 3.0 g salt sample was dissolved in 18.0 mL ultrapure water. For the salted food, 3.0 g sample was soaked in 20.0 mL water for 6 h. Subsequently, they were rinsed 6 times with 10.0 mL ultrapure water each time. After mixing the above soaking solution with the washing solution, the original samples were obtained by centrifugation and filtration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of K4[Fe(CN)6] solid paper sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Specifically, the L-CDs solid sensors were constructed by immersing the filter paper strips in the L-CDs solution for 30 min and allowing to be air-dried. Subsequently, the L-CDs solid sensors were soaked in a series of interfering substance solution with a concentration of 100.0 μmol·L−1 for 30 min. Afterward, the solid sensors were examined under an ultraviolet lamp, and photographs were taken.

First, the lyophilized L-CDs were dissolved in 20.0 mL water to obtain a specific concentration L-CDs solution. Next, 6.6 g of acrylamide (AM) monomer and 0.1% ammonium persulfate (APS) initiator were added to the prepared L-CDs aqueous dispersion. In order to ensure AM and APS could be completely dissolved, the mixture ultrasonic treatment for 10 min at room temperature. Subsequently, nitrogen gas was passed through the solution for 5 min, thereby removing excess gas from the solution. The above prepared solution was then slowly poured into a glass mold at a uniform speed, while the injection port was quickly sealed with plastic wrap to prevent the formation of micro-bubbles. Finally, the glass mold was placed in an oven at 60 °C, and the L-CDs-PAM hydrogel was obtained after 6 h.

The L-CDs were prepared via one-step hydrothermal treatment of L-Try and CC, and a detailed optimization and purification process was then carried out (Figure S1). The TEM image shown that the obtained L-CDs had a circular geometry with an average size of 1.76 ± 0.37 nm determined by counting more than 200 nanoparticles (Fig. 1 a).(Hui Li et al., 2023) The lattice fringes with an average spacing of around 0.21 nm were visible from the HR-TEM image, which could be attributed to the (100) plane of the graphene carbon core (inset Fig. 1a). The AFM image exhibited that the topographic height of L-CDs was 0.9 nm, consistent with TEM analysis results (Fig. 1b).(Lou et al., 2023) As displayed in Fig. 1c, the XRD spectra of L-CDs shown a broad peak at 20°, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphite, suggesting that the carbon crystals had a smaller size and the amorphous nature of L-CDs.(Hui Li et al., 2023) These L-CDs shifts compared to the typical peak (002) of the 26.5° graphite structure were attributed to the formation of surface defects during the solvothermal synthesis.(Sadhanala, Pagidi, & Gedanken, 2021) In the typical Raman spectra, two signal bands could be observed at 1655 cm−1 and 1362 cm−1 (Fig. 1d), corresponding to the graphite G band and the disordered D band. Based on the calculation of relative area of ID/IG, the ratio of sp3/sp2 carbon was 0.99, indicating that the sp2 domain carbonaceous core of L-CDs had a significantly high degree of graphitization.(Hou et al., 2023).

The chemical composition and surface functional groups of the L-CDs were investigated by FT-IR spectra. As exhibited in Fig. 1e, the absorption band at 3434 cm−1 wasattributed to the stretching vibrational bands of OH and NH. The peaks at 2921 cm−1, 1743 cm−1 and 1626 cm−1 could be corresponded to the stretching vibrations of CH, CO and CC, respectively.(Yang et al., 2022) The peak at 1386 cm−1 was associated with the asymmetric stretching vibration of the carboxylate anions, indicating the functionalization of L-Try on L-CDs surface.(Hui Li et al., 2023; Yu et al., 2023) The peaks at 1259 cm−1 coincided ideally with the CN stretching vibration, indicating the existence of amide bonds. These results suggested that there were many functional groups on the surface of L-CDs, such as carboxyl groups and amino groups, which provided the L-CDs with excellent water solubility.(Wei et al., 2023).

Furthermore, the surface analysis and elemental composition of the obtained L-CDs were further investigated via XPS (Fig. 2 d). In the XPS spectrogram, the L-CDs displayed three distinct peaks at 285.0 eV (C1s), 399.0 eV (N1s), and 530.0 eV (O1s), which represented the L-CDs were composed of C (70.15%), N (7.07%) and O (22.78%) elements. The high resolution C1s spectrum was decomposed into four peaks at 284.2 eV, 284.7 eV, 285.9 eV and 288.5 eV, corresponding to CN, CC, CO/CN and COOH, respectively (Fig. 2a).(Ma, Ma, Yang, Tan, Zhao, & Deng, 2023) The highest fitted peak at 284.7 eV for CC/CC further demonstrating the CC component was the dominant carbon-binding configuration, and the L-CDs had graphitic matrix structure.(Na, Chen, Han, Ma, Liu, & Chen, 2019) The N1s spectrum was deconvoluted into three peaks of 398.6 eV (NC), 400.0 eV (NH), and 401.3 eV (NC) (Fig. 2b).(Chen et al., 2023; Lou et al., 2023) Additionally, the high-resolution O1s spectrum was decomposed at two peaks of 531.5 eV (CO) and 532.9 eV (CO), supporting the presence of carboxyl groups on the surface of the L-CDs (Fig. 2c).(Yu et al., 2023).

The composition the L-CDs surface was further characterized using 1H NMR. Interestingly, the aromatic groups were noted in the 1H NMR spectrum in the chemical shift range of 7.0–8.4 ppm, significantly suggesting sp2-hybridized carbon atoms (Fig. 1f).(Yang et al., 2023) Notably, the Ha' atom in the NMR spectrum exhibited the same chemical shift as the Ha atom of L-Try.(Hui Li et al., 2023) These results suggested that the precursor molecules underwent dehydration, polymerization and carbonization to form an aromatic structure while retaining the functional groups of the precursor on the surface of L-CDs.

To discuss the optical features of the L-CDs, fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectroscopy were performed. The L-CDs solution displayed yellowish in daylight and distinctly blue in UV light at 365 nm (inset Fig. 2e). The Fig. 2e exhibited that the absorption peaks of L-CDs at 224 nm and 270 nm were attributed to the π → π* electronic transition of CC.(Na et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2018) Furthermore, the obvious absorption peak at 370 nm was associated with the n → π* transition of CN and CO groups. Next, the emission spectra of the L-CDs at excitation wavelengths from 330 to 400 nm were recorded.(Wang, Wang, et al., 2023) Under the optimal excitation wavelength, the maximum emission peak at 445 nm did not shift with increasing excitation wavelength (Fig. 2f), indicating that the fluorescence emission was independent of excitation due to the mechanism of surface molecular state emission and the stable surface distribution status of L-CDs. At 370 nm excitation, L/D-CDs displayed stronger fluorescence compared with the achiral CDs and L/D-Try-CDs (Figure S2), about 2 times that of L/D-Try-CDs.(Fan et al., 2023; Li et al., 2017) Moreover, the fluorescence quantum yield was measured as 19.2% using water as the reference.(Wei et al., 2023).

Compared with most amino acids, the chiral precursor L/D-Try exhibits a higher melting point (281℃).(Ru et al., 2021) At temperature far below the L/D-Try melting point, the CDs surface with the chiral structure should be well preserved. In order to investigate the chiral features of CDs, the circular dichroic (CD) spectra of L/D-Try-CDs and L/D-CDs were performed. As exhibited in Figure S4, the CD signals of L/D-Try-CDs were symmetric around 220 nm. Evidently, the CD signals of L-CDs and D-CDs around 220 nm inherited the intrinsic chirality of L-Try and D-Try (Figure S5), suggesting that the CDs of L-type and D-type spatial structure were enantiomers.(Ru et al., 2021; Yan, Zhao, Zhang, Zhang, Chen, & Feng, 2023) Furthermore, the weaker CD signal band of L/D-CDs in the wavelength range of 270–350 nm appeared, which might be attributed to the π → π* transition of the sp2-hybridized carbon network in L-CDs. (Di Noja, Amato, Zinna, Di Bari, Ragazzon, & Prato, 2022; Wei, Chen, Wang, Liu, Yang, & Yu, 2019).

It is essential for an outstanding detection system to possess the optimal reaction conditions and strong anti-interference ability. Therefore, detailed experiments including the fluorescence stability of L-CDs at pH values, different temperatures, NaCl concentrations and long storage time were measured to evaluate the detection environment of L-CDs on potassium ferrocyanide. As exhibited in Fig. 3 a, the fluorescence intensity of L-CDs remained unchanged even when the concentration of NaCl was 1.0 mol·L−1, which indicated their probability to recognize targeted objectives in high ionic media. After 100 min of continuous irradiation at 365 nm by an ultraviolet lamp, the fluorescence intensity of L-CDs remained stable, indicating that it had strong photobleaching resistance (Fig. 3b). Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity remained nearly 90% after the storage of solid L-CDs for four months (Figure S6). This property guaranteed the accuracy of potassium ferrocyanide determination for long-term sensing applications. Additionally, significant fluorescence changes were observed from pH 1.0 to 14.0, and this pH-dependent fluorescence behavior was reversible due to surface charge changes during protonation and deprotonation processes, which further supported the carboxyl and amino functionalization of L-CDs (Figures S7, 9).(Zhang, Ding, Zhang, Wang, & Li, 2022) The Figure S8 verified the reversible fluorescence response during 5 running of pH switching processes between pH = 2.0 and 13.0.(Liu et al., 2022) The Figure S10 shown that the fluorescence intensity of L-CDs exhibited a linear decrease as temperature increased from 0℃ to 100℃ (Figure S11). The thermodynamic behavior of L-CDs and the associated thermal decomposition process were investigated by thermal gravimetric analyzer (TGA) and differential thermal analysis (DTG) (Figure S12). The two degradation peaks at about 200℃ and 280℃-600℃ belonged to the pyrolysis of the functional groups on the surface and the carbon network, respectively. Moreover, a large endothermic transition observed prior to 100℃ in the DSC measurement was attributed to the rupture of intramolecular hydrogen bonds (Figure S13).(Wang, He, et al., 2023) These results indicated that the L-CDs had outstanding fluorescence performance as photoluminescent sensors even in various extreme conditions, which would broaden the prospects of application about the chiral L-CDs in several fields, especially sensing.

Because of the strong chemical bonds between the cyanide groups and iron, the ferrocyanides themselves have low toxicity. And they will only decompose at high temperature to produce highly toxic cyanides. Thus, the possibility of fluorescence quenching of L-CDs by cyanide could be ruled out. To verify the selectivity of the sensor to potassium ferrocyanide, the fluorescence intensity of L-CDs to Na+, K+, NH4 +, Cu2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Al3+, F−, Cl−, NO3 –, SO4 2−, K4[Fe(CN)6], Na4[Fe(CN)6], Glu, AA, CA, Cys and PHE in table salt and preserved food were evaluated at 445 nm. As expected, in the existence of potassium ferrocyanide, the fluorescence responding behavior of L-CDs declined clearly to its lowest state, while other metal ions or biological small molecules displayed negligible effects on it. While Na4[Fe(CN)6] also displayed the similar quenching effect on the fluorescence of L-CDs as potassium ferrocyanide, which suggested that ferrocyanide cause the fluorescence quenching of the sensing system in fact (Fig. 3c). Next, a set of co-existing ions including Na+, K+, NH4 +, Cu2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Al3+, F−, Cl−, NO3 –, SO4 2−, Glu, AA, CA, Cys, and PHE were introduced to examine their impact on the fluorescence of the L-CDs-K4[Fe(CN)6] sensor. With the addition of these competing substances, the fluorescence intensity of the L-CDs-K4[Fe(CN)6] system did not change significantly, indicating that the L-CDs had excellent anti-interference performance (Fig. 3d).

To promote the detection ability, we optimized the recognition conditions of L-CDs for potassium ferrocyanide, discovered that the fluorescence response behavior of L-CDs could be rapidly quenched within 20 s upon the addition of 100.0 μL of potassium ferrocyanide (0.0–55.0 μg·mL−1) (Figure S15). The table S1 displayed that the response time of other systems was 1.5–30 times that of the present method. Subsequently, the optimal requirements for sensing potassium ferrocyanide were determined as reaction time of 20 s, and neutral pH for simulating practical samples. Afterwards, the quantification ability of L-CDs for potassium ferrocyanide was evaluated under these factors. As exhibited in Fig. 3e, the fluorescence intensity of L-CDs gradually decreased as the concentration of potassium ferrocyanide (0.0–55.0 μg·mL−1) increased, and the relative intensity of fluorescence quenching F0/F exhibited a significant linear relationship with potassium ferrocyanide concentration (0.0–1.0 μg·mL−1 and 4.0–211.0 μg·mL−1) (Fig. 3f). Compared with the existing detection methods, the L-CDs- K4[Fe(CN)6] system had a wider linear range (Table S1). The linear equation were
F 0
/ F =
( 1.001 ± 0.004 )
+
( 0.352 ± 0.008 )
c
K 4
[
Fe ( C N )
6
]
a n d F
0
/ F =
( 0.965 ± 0.004 )
+
( 0.008 ± 0.003 )
c
K 4
[
Fe ( C N )
6
]
, where c is the concentration of potassium ferrocyanide in μg·mL−1, and the correlation coefficient (R2) were 0.995 and 0.998. Additionally, the estimated LOD based on 3σ/k (σ is the standard deviation of the control, and k is the slope of the calibration curve) were 25.0 ng·mL−1 and 1.2 μg·mL−1.

To evaluate the reliability of the proposed fluorescence sensor, the fluorescence response of L-CDs to potassium ferrocyanide in a real sample was investigated. The L-CDs sensing system was used to analyze initial potassium ferrocyanide concentration levels. Subsequently, the effectiveness of this sensor was assessed by adding three different concentrations of potassium ferrocyanide to table salt and pickled food extraction solution and measuring the recovery rate (Table 1 ). Subsequently, each of samples was added to the L-CDs and the fluorescence spectrums were recorded at 370 nm, and the recovery of potassium ferrocyanide in actual sample was calculated from the measured fluorescence intensity, ranging from 94.6% to 105.5% with the relative standard deviation less than 6.0% (n = 5) (Table 1). Therefore, these L-CDs could serve as a satisfactory sensor to identify potassium ferrocyanide in real life.

To investigate the fluorescence quenching mechanism of L-CDs, the interaction between L-CDs and potassium ferrocyanide was explored to determine whether the quenching process was due to the inner filter effect (IFE) or Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET).(Fang et al., 2022) The absorption spectrum of potassium ferrocyanide exhibited two characteristic absorptions at 270 nm and 325 nm (Fig. 4 a). However, the emission peak of L-CDs appeared at 450 nm when excited at 370 nm, and there is no obvious overlap between them and the absorption spectrum of potassium ferrocyanide, indicated that fluorescence quenching effect of potassium ferrocyanide on L-CDs was not due to IFE or FRET.

The residual effects of fluorescence quenching were investigated after IFE and FRET was excluded. Currently, fluorescence quenching phenomenon were discerned into two dichotomous categories: dynamic and static quenching. The former process is known as collisional quenching between two fluorophores, inducing the dissipation of excitation energy and the consequent return of the excited state fluorophore to the ground state. However, the latter process is induced by the production of a new non-fluorescent complex between quencher molecules and the fluorophores. Therefore, the fluorescence lifetimes of L-CDs and L-CDs-K4[Fe(CN)6] systems were investigated for the possible mechanism. As illustrated in Fig. 4b, the fluorescence lifetime of L-CDs declined from 13.59 ns to 0.48 ns after adding potassium ferrocyanide, indicating that potassium ferrocyanide might quench the fluorescence of L-CDs in a dynamic quenching manner. In addition, the UV absorption spectra of the L-CDs after adding potassium ferrocyanide did not manifest any apparent changes (Figures S16-17), signifying that no new ground-state complex was formed, and the fluorescence quenching of this system involved a dynamic process.

Both the static and the dynamic quenching effect can be theoretically demonstrated by the Stern-Volmer equation Eq. (1)(Li, Gao, Gao, Shan, Bian, & Zhao, 2009)
(1)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
sv
[ Q ]
= 1 +
K q
τ 0
[ Q ]

where F0 was the fluorescence intensity of L-CDs, F was the fluorescence intensity after interaction between L-CDs and potassium ferrocyanide, [Q] was the concentration of quencher potassium ferrocyanide, τ0 was the fluorescence lifetime of L-CDs without quencher, Kq and Ksv were the rate constant in the process of double molecules quenching and the dynamic quenching constant.

The fluorescence quenching effect was explored as a function of temperature. According to Stern-Volmer equation, the values of Ksv were obtained from the linear equations between the FL intensity ratio ((F0-F)/F0) of L-CDs and the concentration of potassium ferrocyanide at different temperatures (Fig. 4c). According to the data in Table S2, the slope of the Stern-Volmer curve rose with the increase of temperature (Ksv (0℃) < Ksv (20℃) < Ksv (37℃)), further demonstrating that the quenching occurred through the dynamic mechanism induced by the collision process between L-CDs and potassium ferrocyanide (Fig. 4d).

To perfectly improve the applicability and efficiency of L-CDs, the solid-phase detectors were prepared. Due to the small size of L-CDs and their excellent optical stability under complex conditions, L-CDs could not only be uniformly distributed in filter paper strips but also be applied to prepare solid-phase sensors in hydrogel grids. As illustrated in Figure S20b-(1), the filter paper soaked with L-CDs showed bright blue fluorescence, which could be quickly quenched by potassium ferrocyanide, while other typical small molecules and metal ions had no noticeable effects on the fluorescence of the L-CDs. Furthermore, the school emblem of Changchun University of Technology was drawn using L-CDs solution, highlighting the application advantages of L-CDs in solid-state sensing (Figure S20b-(2)).(Ai et al., 2023).

Due to the elastic lattice structure of hydrogels, L-CDs were doped into transparent ammonium persulfate-acrylamide hydrogels to synthesize 3D material for sensing experiments. Compared with the pure hydrogels, the L-CDs-doped hydrogels exhibited strong fluorescence, which could be gradually quenched by potassium ferrocyanide within 90 min (Figure S20a), achieving better track and absorption effect on potassium ferrocyanide in water (Figure S18). In a word, the L-CDs retains its excellent properties in the solid phase, which provided the perfect tool for the selective detection of potassium ferrocyanide.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of L-CDs for advanced message encryption
ce:section.ce:para: Information security is an increasingly prominent global hot issue that requires urgent attention, not only in the economic and political aspects, but also in social life.(Lu et al., 2023; Chan Wang et al., 2023) The preparation of information security and cryptographic materials has always been an active area of research. In this paper, novel switchable L-CDs were prepared, and their fluorescence features were utilized for repeated encryption and decryption of information. At first, the required information, such as digit (Figure S20c-a1), English (Figure S20c-b1), Chinese characters (Figure S20c-c1), and shapes (Figure S20c-d1), was pre-written on a piece of paper where nothing could be seen under normal daylight. For the first time, the encrypted content was decrypted by exposing it to 365 nm ultraviolet light. When the potassium ferrocyanide solution was sprayed on paper, the encrypted information was not visible under sunlight or 365 nm UV light (Figure S19) because the fluorescence of L-CDs was quenched by potassium ferrocyanide. After the saturated EDTA solution was sprayed, the blue glow information was recovered under the 365 nm UV lamp. Thus, the next decryption could be achieved. Therefore, this inexpensive, simple, and switchable control strategy supplies a novel idea in the field of information encryption.

In summary, we presented a highly sensitive sensor based on chiral fluorescence L-CDs for the determination of potassium ferrocyanide in complex food matrix. The L-CDs was prepared via a simple ultrafast and green pathway with L-Try and CC as the starting materials, whose fluorescence could be quenched by potassium ferrocyanide through the dynamic quenching process. Furthermore, the L-CDs-K4[Fe(CN)6] system displayed a series of excellent performances, such as time-saving, excellent selectivity and sensitivity, as well as portable feasibility. It was worth noting that the prepared fluorescent sensor had been favorably applied to the analysis of potassium ferrocyanide in salt and pickled food, indicating the application prospect of L-CDs in food quality monitoring. Encouragingly, the L-CDs-doped filter papers and hydrogels could serve as fluorescent solid sensors for efficient and accurate detection of potassium ferrocyanide. Additionally, the reversible fluorescence phenomenon of L-CDs has been successfully applied to information encryption and anti-counterfeiting.

Xiangyu Yin: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Validation, Writing – original draft. Shanshan Wei: Conceptualization, Validation. Changyu Zhai: Validation, Formal analysis. Bin Wang: Conceptualization, Validation. Hongyuan Zhang: Formal analysis. Chenzhao Wang: Conceptualization, Validation. Xuewei Song: Formal analysis, Validation. Guoying Sun: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Chunzhu Jiang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Supervision, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Jilin Province Natural Science Foundation (20220101350JC) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U2241287).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.137207.
